title: EIF4G

infobox.value: (**eIF4G**) is a protein involved in [eukaryotic translation initiation](/wiki/Eukaryotic_translation#Initiation "Eukaryotic translation") and is a component of the [eIF4F](/wiki/Eukaryotic_initiation_factor_4F "Eukaryotic initiation factor 4F") [cap-binding](/wiki/5%27_cap "5' cap") complex. Orthologs of eIF4G have been studied in multiple species, including [humans](/wiki/Humans "Humans"), [yeast](/wiki/Saccharomyces_cerevisiae "Saccharomyces cerevisiae"), and [wheat](/wiki/Wheat "Wheat"). However, eIF4G is exclusively found in [domain](/wiki/Domain_(biology) "Domain (biology)") [Eukarya](/wiki/Eukarya "Eukarya"), and not in domains [Bacteria](/wiki/Bacteria "Bacteria") or [Archaea](/wiki/Archaea "Archaea"), which do not have capped mRNA. As such, eIF4G structure and function may vary between species, although the human [EIF4G1](/wiki/EIF4G1 "EIF4G1") has been the focus of extensive studies. (Other human paralogs are [EIF4G2](/wiki/EIF4G2 "EIF4G2") and [EIF4G3](/wiki/EIF4G3 "EIF4G3").)

is an [initiation factor](/wiki/Initiation_factor "Initiation factor") involved in the assembly of the 43S and 48S translation initiation complex. This particular initiation factor binds to the [PABPI](/wiki/PABPI "PABPI") (PolyA binding protein I), which is in turn binds the [messenger RNA](/wiki/Messenger_RNA "Messenger RNA")'s [poly(A) tail](/wiki/Poly(A)_tail "Poly(A) tail") and [eIF3](/wiki/Eukaryotic_initiation_factor_3 "Eukaryotic initiation factor 3"), which is bound to the incoming [small ribosomal subunit (40S)](/wiki/Eukaryotic_small_ribosomal_subunit_(40S) "Eukaryotic small ribosomal subunit (40S)").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Orthologs of eIF4G have been studied in multiple species, including [humans](/wiki/Humans "Humans"), [yeast](/wiki/Saccharomyces_cerevisiae "Saccharomyces cerevisiae"), and [wheat](/wiki/Wheat "Wheat"). However, eIF4G is exclusively found in [domain](/wiki/Domain_(biology) "Domain (biology)") [Eukarya](/wiki/Eukarya "Eukarya"), and not in domains [Bacteria](/wiki/Bacteria "Bacteria") or [Archaea](/wiki/Archaea "Archaea"), which do not have capped mRNA. As such, eIF4G structure and function may vary between species, although the human [EIF4G1](/wiki/EIF4G1 "EIF4G1") has been the focus of extensive studies. (Other human paralogs are [EIF4G2](/wiki/EIF4G2 "EIF4G2") and [EIF4G3](/wiki/EIF4G3 "EIF4G3").)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Protein family

| Initiation factor 4G | |
| --- | --- |
| Identifiers | |
| Symbol | IF4G |
| [InterPro](/wiki/InterPro "InterPro") | [IPR045208](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/interpro/entry/IPR045208) |
| | Available protein structures: | | | --- | --- | | [PDB](/wiki/Protein_Data_Bank "Protein Data Bank") | [IPR045208](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/interpro/entry/InterPro/IPR045208/structure/PDB) | | [AlphaFold](/wiki/AlphaFold "AlphaFold") | * [IPR045208](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/interpro/entry/InterPro/IPR045208/alphafold/) | | |

**Eukaryotic translation initiation factor 4 G** (**eIF4G**) is a protein involved in [eukaryotic translation initiation](/wiki/Eukaryotic_translation#Initiation "Eukaryotic translation") and is a component of the [eIF4F](/wiki/Eukaryotic_initiation_factor_4F "Eukaryotic initiation factor 4F") [cap-binding](/wiki/5%27_cap "5' cap") complex. Orthologs of eIF4G have been studied in multiple species, including [humans](/wiki/Humans "Humans"), [yeast](/wiki/Saccharomyces_cerevisiae "Saccharomyces cerevisiae"), and [wheat](/wiki/Wheat "Wheat"). However, eIF4G is exclusively found in [domain](/wiki/Domain_(biology) "Domain (biology)") [Eukarya](/wiki/Eukarya "Eukarya"), and not in domains [Bacteria](/wiki/Bacteria "Bacteria") or [Archaea](/wiki/Archaea "Archaea"), which do not have capped mRNA. As such, eIF4G structure and function may vary between species, although the human [EIF4G1](/wiki/EIF4G1 "EIF4G1") has been the focus of extensive studies. (Other human paralogs are [EIF4G2](/wiki/EIF4G2 "EIF4G2") and [EIF4G3](/wiki/EIF4G3 "EIF4G3").)

Across species, eIF4G strongly associates with [eIF4E](/wiki/EIF4E "EIF4E"), the protein that directly binds the mRNA cap. Together with the [RNA helicase](/wiki/Helicase "Helicase") protein [eIF4A](/wiki/EIF4A "EIF4A"), these form the [eIF4F](/wiki/Eukaryotic_initiation_factor_4F "Eukaryotic initiation factor 4F") complex.

Within the cell eIF4G is found primarily in the cytoplasm, usually bound to eIF4E; however, it is also found in the nucleus, where its function is unknown. It may have a role in [nonsense-mediated decay](/wiki/Nonsense-mediated_decay "Nonsense-mediated decay").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=EIF4G&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

eIF4G stands for eukaryotic initiation factor 4 gamma (typically gamma is now replaced by G in the literature). It was initially isolated by [fractionation](/wiki/Fractionation "Fractionation"), found present in fraction 4 gamma, and was involved in [eukaryotic translation initiation](/wiki/Eukaryotic_translation#Initiation "Eukaryotic translation").

## Binding partners

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=EIF4G&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Binding partners")]

eIF4G has been found to associate with many other proteins besides those of the [eIF4F](/wiki/EIF4F "EIF4F") complex, including MNK-1, CBP80, CBP20, [PABP](/wiki/Poly(A)-binding_protein "Poly(A)-binding protein"), and [eIF3](/wiki/EIF3 "EIF3"). eIF4G also directly binds mRNA and has multiple positively charged regions for this function. Several [IRESs](/wiki/Internal_ribosome_entry_site "Internal ribosome entry site") also bind eIF4G directly, as do [BTE CITEs](/wiki/Luteovirus_cap-independent_translation_element "Luteovirus cap-independent translation element").

## In translation initiation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=EIF4G&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: In translation initiation")]

eIF4G is an important scaffold for the eIF4F complex and aids in recruiting the [40S ribosomal subunit](/wiki/Eukaryotic_small_ribosomal_subunit_(40S) "Eukaryotic small ribosomal subunit (40S)") to mRNA.

There are three mechanisms that the 40S ribosome can come to recognize the start codon: scanning, internal entry, and [shunting](/wiki/Ribosome_shunting "Ribosome shunting"). In scanning, the 40S ribosome slides along the RNA until it recognizes a start site (typically an AUG sequence in "good context"). In internal entry, the 40S ribosome does not start from the beginning (5' end) of the mRNA but instead starts from somewhere in the middle. In shunting, after the 40S ribosome starts sliding along the mRNA it "jumps" or skips large sections; the mechanism for this is still unclear. eIF4G is required for most types of initiation, except in special cases such as internal initiation at the [HCV IRES](/wiki/HCV_IRES "HCV IRES") or [Cripavirus IRES](/wiki/Cripavirus_IRES "Cripavirus IRES").

**eIF4G** is an [initiation factor](/wiki/Initiation_factor "Initiation factor") involved in the assembly of the 43S and 48S translation initiation complex. This particular initiation factor binds to the [PABPI](/wiki/PABPI "PABPI") (PolyA binding protein I), which is in turn binds the [messenger RNA](/wiki/Messenger_RNA "Messenger RNA")'s [poly(A) tail](/wiki/Poly(A)_tail "Poly(A) tail") and [eIF3](/wiki/Eukaryotic_initiation_factor_3 "Eukaryotic initiation factor 3"), which is bound to the incoming [small ribosomal subunit (40S)](/wiki/Eukaryotic_small_ribosomal_subunit_(40S) "Eukaryotic small ribosomal subunit (40S)").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## In disease

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=EIF4G&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: In disease")]

eIF4G has been implicated in breast cancer. It appears in increased levels in certain types of breast cancer and increases production of mRNAs that contain [IRESs](/wiki/Internal_ribosome_entry_site "Internal ribosome entry site"); these mRNAs produce hypoxia- and stress-related proteins that encourage blood vessel invasion (which is important for tumorigenesis).

## Role in aging

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=EIF4G&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Role in aging")]

Regulation of translation initiation by eIF4G is vital for protein synthesis in developing organisms, for example yeast and nematodes. Deletion of eIF4G is lethal in yeast.[[2]](#cite_note-2) In the roundworm [C. elegans](/wiki/Caenorhabditis_elegans "Caenorhabditis elegans"), knockout of eIF4G leads to animals that cannot develop past the early larval stage (L2) of development.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The critical role of eIF4G in development appears to be reversed in adulthood, when eIF4G dysregulation negatively impacts lifespan and increases susceptibility to certain aging-related diseases (see eIF4G in diseases above). Inhibiting eIF4G during adulthood in C. elegans drastically extends lifespan, comparable to the lifespan increase exhibited during dietary restriction.[[4]](#cite_note-4) In addition, inhibiting eIF4G reduces overall protein translation, while preferentially translating mRNA of genes important for responding to stress and against those associated with growth and reproduction.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Thus eIF4G appears to control differential mRNA translation during periods or growth and stress, which may ultimately lead to age-related decline.

## Importance in virology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=EIF4G&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Importance in virology")]

As previously mentioned, eIF4G is bound by certain IRESs, which were initially discovered in viruses. Some viral IRESs directly bind eIF4G and co-opt it to gain access to the ribosome. Some cellular mRNAs also contain IRESs (including eIF4G itself).[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Some viral proteases cleave off part of eIF4G, that contains the eIF4E binding region. This has the effect of preventing most cellular mRNAs from binding eIF4G; however, a few cellular mRNAs with IRESs still translate under these conditions.

One example of an eIF4G binding site in a viral IRES is in the EMCV IRES (nucleotides 746–949).[[7]](#cite_note-pmid-7)
